items.prompt_md: In the 0/1 knapsack table, `dp[i][s]` is true if `dp[i-1][s]` is true (skip item i-1), OR if which other cell is true?
options: dp[i-1][s - nums[i-1]], when nums[i-1] <= s
dp[i-1][s + nums[i-1]]
dp[i][s - 1]
items.answer: dp[i-1][s - nums[i-1]], when nums[i-1] <= s
items.explanation_md: Taking item i-1 means sum s was reachable if s minus this item's value was reachable using only the earlier items.

items.prompt_md: What does dp[i][s] represent in the 0/1 knapsack subset-sum table?
options: Whether sum s is reachable using only the first i items
The maximum sum reachable using the first i items
The number of subsets of the first i items that sum to s
Whether item i alone equals s
items.answer: Whether sum s is reachable using only the first i items
items.explanation_md: dp[i][s] is a boolean reachability question scoped to a prefix of items and a specific target sum — not a count and not a max.

items.prompt_md: Why is dp[i][0] always true for every i in the knapsack subset-sum table?
options: Sum 0 is always reachable by taking none of the items, regardless of how many items are available
Because item 0 always has value 0
Because the table is initialized to true everywhere by default
Because taking every item always sums to 0 in this formulation
items.answer: Sum 0 is always reachable by taking none of the items, regardless of how many items are available
items.explanation_md: The empty subset always sums to 0, so target 0 is trivially reachable no matter which prefix of items you're allowed to use — this is the base case the recurrence builds on.

items.prompt_md: Why must you check `nums[i-1] <= s` before reading dp[i-1][s - nums[i-1]] in the knapsack recurrence?
options: To avoid indexing the table with a negative remaining sum, which is not a valid state
To make sure the item's value is even
To ensure items are processed in sorted order
Because dp[i-1][s] is undefined otherwise
items.answer: To avoid indexing the table with a negative remaining sum, which is not a valid state
items.explanation_md: If the item's value exceeds the remaining capacity s, "taking it" would require a negative remaining sum, which isn't a state the table represents — so that branch is simply skipped.

items.prompt_md: Why does 0/1 knapsack collapse the search space from 2^n subsets down to n * target reachable states?
options: Because the decision for item i only depends on how many items have been considered and how much capacity remains — not on which specific earlier items were chosen
Because sorting the items first removes most of the branching
Because only even-valued items need to be considered
Because duplicate items are automatically merged
items.answer: Because the decision for item i only depends on how many items have been considered and how much capacity remains — not on which specific earlier items were chosen
items.explanation_md: Many different subsets can lead to the same (items-considered, remaining-capacity) state, and the DP table only needs to track that state, not the specific path taken to reach it.

items.prompt_md: Why must you iterate the weight/sum dimension from high to low when rolling the 0/1 knapsack table down to a 1-D array?
options: Because updating from low to high would let an item be counted more than once, reading a value already updated in the current pass
Because high-to-low iteration is faster on modern CPUs
Because the target sum must always be a power of two
It doesn't matter which direction you iterate, as long as you finish the row
items.answer: Because updating from low to high would let an item be counted more than once, reading a value already updated in the current pass
items.explanation_md: Going low to high, dp[s - nums[i-1]] might already reflect this same item being taken earlier in the same pass, effectively allowing unlimited reuse; going high to low guarantees you read last row's value.

items.prompt_md: In the partition-equal-subset-sum problem, why is the target computed as total // 2, and why does an odd total immediately return false?
options: Two equal-sum partitions must each sum to exactly half the total, and an odd total can't be split into two equal integer halves
Because integer division is always required before any DP table is built
Because the array must always contain an even number of elements
Because dp tables only work with even targets
items.answer: Two equal-sum partitions must each sum to exactly half the total, and an odd total can't be split into two equal integer halves
items.explanation_md: If the two partitions must have equal sums, each must be total/2; an odd total makes that impossible before any DP work is needed.

items.prompt_md: How does the 0/1 knapsack table's column meaning differ from the grid-path table's column meaning, even though both are 2-D tables filled the same way?
options: Knapsack's columns index remaining target/capacity, while grid-path's columns index physical position in the grid
They mean exactly the same thing in both problems
Knapsack's columns index the item's own weight only
Grid-path's columns index remaining capacity, same as knapsack
items.answer: Knapsack's columns index remaining target/capacity, while grid-path's columns index physical position in the grid
items.explanation_md: The two tables share the same fill discipline (seed a base row, respect dependency order) but the axes carry different meanings — knapsack's second axis is a resource budget, not a spatial coordinate.

items.prompt_md: A common bug in 0/1 knapsack is forgetting to bound-check the 'take' branch. What happens if you compute dp[i-1][s - nums[i-1]] without checking nums[i-1] <= s first?
options: You'd index the table with a negative sum, which either crashes or silently wraps to the wrong cell
The answer is still correct because the skip branch always dominates
It only affects the final row, not intermediate results
Nothing changes, since dp[i-1][negative] is defined as false everywhere
items.answer: You'd index the table with a negative sum, which either crashes or silently wraps to the wrong cell
items.explanation_md: A negative index isn't a valid dp state; without the guard, code either throws an out-of-range error or (in languages that allow negative indexing) silently reads the wrong cell and corrupts the result.

items.prompt_md: In a variant asking for the number of subsets that sum to a target (rather than just whether one exists), how does the recurrence change from the boolean knapsack table?
options: dp[i][s] = dp[i-1][s] + dp[i-1][s - nums[i-1]] (sum the ways from skip and take) instead of OR-ing two booleans
The recurrence is identical; only the final answer's type changes from bool to int
dp[i][s] = max(dp[i-1][s], dp[i-1][s - nums[i-1]])
dp[i][s] = dp[i-1][s] * dp[i-1][s - nums[i-1]]
items.answer: dp[i][s] = dp[i-1][s] + dp[i-1][s - nums[i-1]] (sum the ways from skip and take) instead of OR-ing two booleans
items.explanation_md: Counting the number of ways requires summing the ways from each branch instead of just OR-ing true/false, since multiple distinct subsets can reach the same sum.

items.prompt_md: What is the time and space complexity of the 0/1 knapsack table for n items and a target sum T?
options: O(n * T) time and space
O(2^n) time and space
O(n + T) time and space
O(n * T) time, O(1) space always
items.answer: O(n * T) time and space
items.explanation_md: Every one of the n*T cells is filled once from a constant number of neighbors, so the fill is O(n*T) time and space (pseudo-polynomial, since T can be large).

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of the brute-force approach that tries every subset of n items to check for a target sum?
options: O(n * T)
O(n log n)
items.explanation_md: There are 2^n possible subsets, and checking each one costs at least O(n), making brute force exponential — the DP table replaces this with O(n*T) pseudo-polynomial work.

items.prompt_md: After rolling the 0/1 knapsack table down to a single 1-D array of size T+1, what is the space complexity?
options: O(T)
O(n)
O(n * T)
items.answer: O(T)
items.explanation_md: Only one row (indexed by remaining sum) needs to exist at a time, since each row only depends on the row directly above it — updated carefully in the high-to-low direction.

items.prompt_md: Why is O(n * T) called 'pseudo-polynomial' rather than truly polynomial in the size of the input?
options: Because T is a numeric value that can be exponentially large relative to the number of bits used to encode it, unlike n which is a count of items
Because the algorithm actually runs in exponential time despite the formula
Because T is always equal to n in knapsack problems
Because pseudo-polynomial just means the same thing as polynomial
items.answer: Because T is a numeric value that can be exponentially large relative to the number of bits used to encode it, unlike n which is a count of items
items.explanation_md: T's magnitude, not its bit-length, drives the runtime — so for T values that grow exponentially in the number of bits needed to represent them, O(n*T) is not polynomial in input size.

items.prompt_md: For the unbounded knapsack variant (each item usable unlimited times), what is the time complexity of filling the same-sized dp table, assuming the fill order is adjusted appropriately?
options: O(n * T), the same asymptotic cost as 0/1 knapsack
O(2^n * T), since unlimited reuse multiplies the search space
O(n + T)
items.answer: O(n * T), the same asymptotic cost as 0/1 knapsack
items.explanation_md: Allowing unlimited reuse only changes which row/direction you read from during the fill (enabling reuse within the same item), not the number of cells computed — it's still n*T cells done in constant work each.

items.prompt_md: You're given an array of numbers and asked whether some subset of them sums to exactly a target, where each number may be used at most once. Which approach fits?
options: Sort the array and use two pointers to find a pair
Build a 2-D table dp[i][s] over items-considered x remaining-sum, branching on include/exclude
Slide a variable-size window across the array
Count digit frequencies with a hash map
items.answer: Build a 2-D table dp[i][s] over items-considered x remaining-sum, branching on include/exclude
items.explanation_md: "Each item used at most once, hit an exact target sum" is the fingerprint of 0/1 knapsack — a 2-D table over (items so far, remaining target).

items.prompt_md: You're given an array of positive integers and must determine if it can be split into two subsets with equal sums, each element used exactly once overall. Which approach fits?
options: Reduce to 0/1 knapsack: check if half the total sum is reachable using a 2-D include/exclude table
Sort the array and greedily assign the largest remaining element to whichever subset has the smaller sum
Backtracking over every possible two-way split
A 1-D DP array indexed only by position in the array
items.answer: Reduce to 0/1 knapsack: check if half the total sum is reachable using a 2-D include/exclude table
items.explanation_md: Equal-subset partition is exactly subset-sum with target = total/2 — a capacity-style target with each item usable once is the 0/1 knapsack trigger; a greedy largest-first assignment can provably fail on some inputs.

items.prompt_md: You're given coin denominations and a target amount, and asked for the number of ways to make that amount, where each denomination can be used an unlimited number of times. Which approach fits?
options: A 2-D-shaped DP over (denominations considered, remaining amount), but iterating the amount forward since reuse is allowed
0/1 knapsack unchanged, iterating the amount backward as if each coin were single-use
Backtracking over every combination of coins
A greedy approach that always uses the largest coin that fits
items.answer: A 2-D-shaped DP over (denominations considered, remaining amount), but iterating the amount forward since reuse is allowed
items.explanation_md: This is unbounded knapsack, a close cousin: same axes (items x remaining target) but each row can reuse itself, so the amount dimension is scanned forward rather than backward like 0/1 knapsack.

items.prompt_md: You're given a single array and asked for the maximum sum of a non-empty subsequence where no two chosen elements are adjacent in the original array (a classic 'no two adjacent' constraint, one array, one linear axis). Which approach fits?
options: A 1-D DP array where dp[i] is the best sum using the first i elements, taking or skipping element i
A 2-D DP table over items x target sum, exactly like 0/1 knapsack
Backtracking over every subset that avoids adjacent picks
A greedy pass always taking the larger of every adjacent pair
items.answer: A 1-D DP array where dp[i] is the best sum using the first i elements, taking or skipping element i
items.explanation_md: This is a trap: although it's a take-or-skip decision like knapsack, there's no separate capacity/target dimension — the only changing quantity is position in the array, so a 1-D DP array (not a 2-D capacity table) is the right shape.

items.prompt_md: You're given a set of items each with a weight and a value, and a knapsack capacity; you want to maximize total value without exceeding capacity, each item usable at most once. Which approach fits?
options: 2-D DP table dp[i][w] over items x capacity, taking the max of skip-value and take-value
Greedy: sort by value-to-weight ratio and take items until capacity runs out
Backtracking over every subset of items
A 1-D DP array indexed only by item index, ignoring capacity
items.answer: 2-D DP table dp[i][w] over items x capacity, taking the max of skip-value and take-value
items.explanation_md: This is the textbook 0/1 knapsack value-maximization form; the greedy value-density heuristic is correct for the fractional version of knapsack but can be provably suboptimal once items must be taken whole.

items.prompt_md: You're given an array of positive integers and a target, and must count the number of ways to assign a '+' or '-' sign to each number so the signed sum equals the target, each number used exactly once. Which approach fits?
options: Reduce to a 0/1 knapsack counting variant: reframe as choosing a subset to receive '+' that sums to a derived target
Backtracking over every sign assignment (only feasible for tiny inputs)
A greedy pass assigning '+' to the larger numbers and '-' to the smaller ones
A 1-D DP array over just the running sum
items.answer: Reduce to a 0/1 knapsack counting variant: reframe as choosing a subset to receive '+' that sums to a derived target
items.explanation_md: Sign-assignment with a target sum algebraically reduces to subset-sum counting (each item used once, hitting a derived target), so it inherits the 0/1 knapsack 2-D table — plain backtracking works but scales exponentially instead.

items.prompt_md: You're given a small array (n <= 12) of items with weights and values and a capacity, and asked to output the actual optimal subset of items, not just its value. Which approach fits?
options: Backtracking / try all — enumerate every subset explicitly and track the best one, since n is small enough
0/1 knapsack DP table alone, since it already stores every optimal subset
A greedy value-density heuristic to pick the subset directly
A 1-D DP array over capacity only, ignoring which items were chosen
items.answer: Backtracking / try all — enumerate every subset explicitly and track the best one, since n is small enough
items.explanation_md: This is a trap: with n this small, brute-force enumeration is simple and fast enough, and it directly hands you the actual subset — the DP table alone gives you the optimal value but reconstructing the exact subset from it is extra bookkeeping that backtracking gets for free at this scale.

items.prompt_md: You're given an array of stone weights and must split them into two groups to minimize the absolute difference between the two groups' total weights. Which approach fits?
options: 0/1 knapsack table checking which sums up to total/2 are reachable, then picking the reachable sum closest to it
Sort the stones and alternate assigning them to each group
A 1-D DP array over the number of stones only
Backtracking over every two-way split (fine for any input size)
items.answer: 0/1 knapsack table checking which sums up to total/2 are reachable, then picking the reachable sum closest to it
items.explanation_md: Minimizing the difference between two group sums is subset-sum in disguise: find the reachable sum closest to half the total, each stone used exactly once — the 0/1 knapsack reachability table answers exactly that.

items.prompt_md: You're given a list of jobs, each with a start time, end time, and profit, and must select a subset of non-overlapping jobs to maximize total profit. Which approach fits?
options: Sort jobs by end time and use DP where dp[i] takes the best of skipping job i or taking it plus the best compatible earlier job
0/1 knapsack table over (jobs considered, remaining time capacity)
Backtracking over every subset of jobs
A greedy pass always picking the job with the highest profit first
items.answer: Sort jobs by end time and use DP where dp[i] takes the best of skipping job i or taking it plus the best compatible earlier job
items.explanation_md: This is a trap: even though it's a take-or-skip decision per item like knapsack, the constraint is time-overlap, not a shared numeric capacity budget — after sorting by end time, the state collapses to a single index (job position), so it's 1-D DP (weighted interval scheduling), not a 2-D capacity table.

items.prompt_md: You're given an array of positive integers and a capacity, and must find the maximum number of items (not the maximum value) you can fit without exceeding the capacity, each item usable at most once. Which approach fits?
options: 2-D DP table dp[i][w] over items x capacity, maximizing item count instead of value
Greedy: sort by weight ascending and keep adding the smallest remaining items until capacity is exhausted
Backtracking over every subset of items
A 1-D DP array over item index only, ignoring capacity
items.answer: Greedy: sort by weight ascending and keep adding the smallest remaining items until capacity is exhausted
items.explanation_md: This is a trap: when maximizing the *count* of items under a capacity (not their value), taking the smallest weights first is provably optimal, so a sort-and-greedy-sweep beats building a full 2-D DP table.
